---
title: "Research"
description: "CompOmics research themes: peptide and protein identification, AI-based spectrum and retention-time prediction, and FAIR proteomics data."
toc: false
---

```{=html}
<header class="research-header">
  <div class="research-header-kicker">What we do</div>
  <h1 class="research-header-title">Research</h1>
  <p class="research-header-sub">We are a team of 26 enthusiastic computational biologists, bioinformaticians, and software developers who build methods and tools across the proteomics workflow. Our work spans peptide property prediction, mass spectrometry data analysis, data management, metadata standardization, and the large-scale reuse of public proteomics data.</p>
  <p class="research-header-text">Modern <strong>mass spectrometry</strong> can measure proteins with a unseen depth and throughput that increasingly exceeds our ability to interpret the resulting data. CompOmics works to close this gap. We develop open-source software, machine learning models, web resources, and community-oriented data standards that make proteomics data analysis <strong>faster</strong>, more <strong>accurate</strong>, and more <strong>reproducible</strong>. By keeping our tools and data resources openly available, we aim to support transparent, reusable, and scalable proteomics research for the wider community.</p>

  <!-- Signature: the five research lines drawn as a five-channel mass spectrum.
       Peaks are evenly spaced at 10/30/50/70/90% so the clickable labels below
       align exactly under them via a five-column grid. -->
  <figure class="research-spectrum">
    <svg viewBox="0 0 960 224" preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMax meet" aria-hidden="true">
      <!-- axes: intensity (y) vs m/z (x), so the motif reads as a mass spectrum -->
      <line class="spec-axis" x1="24" y1="16" x2="24" y2="180" />
      <polygon class="spec-axis-arrow" points="24,12 20,20 28,20" />
      <line class="spec-base" x1="24" y1="180" x2="940" y2="180" />
      <polygon class="spec-axis-arrow" points="944,180 936,176 936,184" />
      <text class="spec-axis-label" x="486" y="210" text-anchor="middle">m/z</text>
      <text class="spec-axis-label" x="13" y="98" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90 13 98)">Intensity</text>

      <!-- faint minor peaks: chaotic spectrum texture (never animated). Greyed
           out, so some can out-tower the accent peaks without stealing focus. -->
      <g class="spec-minor">
        <rect x="28" y="171" width="2" height="9" /><rect x="36" y="140" width="3" height="40" /><rect x="43" y="174" width="2" height="6" /><rect x="55" y="156" width="3" height="24" /><rect x="62" y="170" width="2" height="10" /><rect x="72" y="110" width="3" height="70" /><rect x="80" y="175" width="2" height="5" />
        <rect x="104" y="168" width="2" height="12" /><rect x="112" y="125" width="3" height="55" /><rect x="120" y="173" width="2" height="7" /><rect x="129" y="164" width="2" height="16" /><rect x="138" y="85" width="3" height="95" /><rect x="147" y="174" width="2" height="6" /><rect x="156" y="167" width="2" height="13" /><rect x="166" y="160" width="3" height="20" /><rect x="176" y="171" width="2" height="9" /><rect x="186" y="45" width="3" height="135" /><rect x="196" y="173" width="2" height="7" /><rect x="206" y="163" width="2" height="17" /><rect x="217" y="169" width="2" height="11" /><rect x="228" y="174" width="2" height="6" /><rect x="240" y="132" width="3" height="48" /><rect x="251" y="171" width="2" height="9" /><rect x="262" y="166" width="2" height="14" /><rect x="273" y="173" width="2" height="7" />
        <rect x="298" y="167" width="2" height="13" /><rect x="308" y="120" width="3" height="60" /><rect x="318" y="172" width="2" height="8" /><rect x="329" y="164" width="2" height="16" /><rect x="339" y="35" width="3" height="145" /><rect x="350" y="173" width="2" height="7" /><rect x="360" y="168" width="2" height="12" /><rect x="371" y="150" width="3" height="30" /><rect x="382" y="174" width="2" height="6" /><rect x="393" y="165" width="2" height="15" /><rect x="404" y="142" width="3" height="38" /><rect x="415" y="171" width="2" height="9" /><rect x="427" y="163" width="2" height="17" /><rect x="438" y="173" width="2" height="7" /><rect x="450" y="160" width="3" height="20" /><rect x="461" y="170" width="2" height="10" />
        <rect x="490" y="168" width="2" height="12" /><rect x="501" y="100" width="3" height="80" /><rect x="512" y="172" width="2" height="8" /><rect x="524" y="161" width="2" height="19" /><rect x="535" y="70" width="3" height="110" /><rect x="546" y="173" width="2" height="7" /><rect x="557" y="166" width="2" height="14" /><rect x="569" y="171" width="2" height="9" /><rect x="581" y="128" width="3" height="52" /><rect x="592" y="169" width="2" height="11" /><rect x="604" y="164" width="2" height="16" /><rect x="616" y="173" width="2" height="7" /><rect x="629" y="40" width="3" height="140" /><rect x="641" y="171" width="2" height="9" /><rect x="653" y="165" width="2" height="15" />
        <rect x="682" y="170" width="2" height="10" /><rect x="693" y="136" width="3" height="44" /><rect x="704" y="30" width="3" height="150" /><rect x="715" y="173" width="2" height="7" /><rect x="727" y="167" width="2" height="13" /><rect x="739" y="162" width="2" height="18" /><rect x="751" y="114" width="3" height="66" /><rect x="763" y="169" width="2" height="11" /><rect x="776" y="164" width="2" height="16" /><rect x="788" y="173" width="2" height="7" /><rect x="801" y="145" width="3" height="35" /><rect x="813" y="171" width="2" height="9" /><rect x="826" y="90" width="3" height="90" /><rect x="838" y="166" width="2" height="14" /><rect x="851" y="173" width="2" height="7" />
        <rect x="874" y="169" width="2" height="11" /><rect x="885" y="122" width="3" height="58" /><rect x="897" y="165" width="2" height="15" /><rect x="909" y="50" width="3" height="130" /><rect x="920" y="173" width="2" height="7" /><rect x="931" y="167" width="2" height="13" />
      </g>

      <!-- five accent peaks, one per research line (labelled by the nav below).
           The line initials spell the peptide AIMFR, so the peaks sit at the
           m/z of its b-ion ladder (b1..b4 + precursor). Same height for all. -->
      <g class="spec-peak" style="--i:0; color:#1f5f8f"><rect x="120" y="50" width="10" height="130" rx="3" /></g>
      <g class="spec-peak" style="--i:1; color:#c5392b"><rect x="271" y="50" width="10" height="130" rx="3" /></g>
      <g class="spec-peak" style="--i:2; color:#2f8f6f"><rect x="445" y="50" width="10" height="130" rx="3" /></g>
      <g class="spec-peak" style="--i:3; color:#b0792a"><rect x="641" y="50" width="10" height="130" rx="3" /></g>
      <g class="spec-peak" style="--i:4; color:#6a4c93"><rect x="873" y="50" width="10" height="130" rx="3" /></g>

      <!-- residue ladder: double-headed arrows between successive b-ions; each
           gap is one residue mass, so the arrows spell A·I·M·F·R across the peaks -->
      <g class="spec-annot">
        <line x1="139" y1="37" x2="262" y2="37" /><polygon points="125,37 133,34 133,40" /><polygon points="276,37 268,34 268,40" /><text class="spec-annot-res" x="200" y="30">I</text><text class="spec-annot-mz" x="200" y="47">113.08</text>
        <line x1="290" y1="37" x2="436" y2="37" /><polygon points="276,37 284,34 284,40" /><polygon points="450,37 442,34 442,40" /><text class="spec-annot-res" x="363" y="30">M</text><text class="spec-annot-mz" x="363" y="47">131.04</text>
        <line x1="464" y1="37" x2="632" y2="37" /><polygon points="450,37 458,34 458,40" /><polygon points="646,37 638,34 638,40" /><text class="spec-annot-res" x="548" y="30">F</text><text class="spec-annot-mz" x="548" y="47">147.07</text>
        <line x1="660" y1="37" x2="864" y2="37" /><polygon points="646,37 654,34 654,40" /><polygon points="878,37 870,34 870,40" /><text class="spec-annot-res" x="762" y="30">R</text><text class="spec-annot-mz" x="762" y="47">156.10</text>
      </g>
    </svg>
    <nav class="research-spectrum-nav" aria-label="Research lines">
      <a class="rs-line" style="--accent:#1f5f8f; --i:0; left:13.0%" href="#analyte-prediction">Analyte prediction</a>
      <a class="rs-line" style="--accent:#c5392b; --i:1; left:28.8%" href="#identification">Identification</a>
      <a class="rs-line" style="--accent:#2f8f6f; --i:2; left:46.9%" href="#metaproteomics">Metaproteomics</a>
      <a class="rs-line" style="--accent:#b0792a; --i:3; left:67.3%" href="#fair-ai-ready-data">FAIR &amp; AI-ready data</a>
      <a class="rs-line" style="--accent:#6a4c93; --i:4; left:91.5%" href="#reprocessing">Reprocessing</a>
    </nav>
  </figure>

  <a class="research-scroll-cue" href="#analyte-prediction" aria-label="Scroll to the research lines">
    <span>Explore</span>
    <i class="bi bi-chevron-down" aria-hidden="true"></i>
  </a>
</header>

<div class="research-sections">

  <section class="research-section" id="analyte-prediction" style="--accent:#1f5f8f">
    <div class="research-section-top">
      <span class="research-peak" aria-hidden="true"><svg viewBox="0 0 30 56" preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMax meet"><line class="peak-base" x1="0" y1="55" x2="30" y2="55" /><rect class="peak-bar" x="12" y="8" width="6" height="47" rx="2" /></svg></span>
      <h2 class="research-section-title">Analyte prediction</h2>
    </div>
    <p class="research-section-desc">Proteomics becomes more powerful when we can predict what a peptide should look like before it is measured. We develop machine learning models that translate peptide sequences into expected mass spectrometry behaviour, including fragment ion intensities, retention time, and ion mobility. Tools such as MS²PIP, DeepLC, and IM2Deep make these predicted signals available for practical analysis workflows. This work supports spectral library generation, targeted method design, and identification rescoring, but its broader aim is more fundamental: to make peptide behaviour computationally explicit, testable, and reusable across instruments, datasets, and experimental designs.</p>
  </section>

  <section class="research-section" id="identification" style="--accent:#c5392b">
    <div class="research-section-top">
      <span class="research-peak" aria-hidden="true"><svg viewBox="0 0 30 56" preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMax meet"><line class="peak-base" x1="0" y1="55" x2="30" y2="55" /><rect class="peak-bar" x="12" y="8" width="6" height="47" rx="2" /></svg></span>
      <h2 class="research-section-title">Identification</h2>
    </div>
    <p class="research-section-desc">Peptide and protein identification is not only a database search problem; it is an ambiguity problem. A first-pass search engine proposes candidate matches, but much of the information in the data remains underused. We develop post-processing and data-driven rescoring approaches that combine search engine scores with predicted peptide behaviour, spectrum evidence, retention time, and ion mobility information. Through tools such as MS²Rescore/TIMS²Rescore combined with SearchGUI & PeptideShaker, we aim to increase confident identifications in DDA and DIA workflows while keeping false discovery control central. Our goal is to recover reliable signal without compromising statistical confidence.</p>
  </section>

  <section class="research-section" id="metaproteomics" style="--accent:#2f8f6f">
    <div class="research-section-top">
      <span class="research-peak" aria-hidden="true"><svg viewBox="0 0 30 56" preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMax meet"><line class="peak-base" x1="0" y1="55" x2="30" y2="55" /><rect class="peak-bar" x="12" y="8" width="6" height="47" rx="2" /></svg></span>
      <h2 class="research-section-title">Metaproteomics</h2>
    </div>
    <p class="research-section-desc">Metaproteomics asks which organisms are present in a microbial community, what they are doing, and how they are doing it and importantly how confidently this can be inferred from peptide evidence. This is difficult because many peptides are shared across related taxa, while many functions are distributed across complex and unevenly characterized microbial proteomes. We develop methods for taxonomic and functional interpretation that explicitly account for this ambiguity rather than hiding it. Tools such as Unipept and peptonizer 2000 help translate peptide-level evidence into interpretable community profiles. We want to make metaproteomics scalable, transparent, and statistically grounded, from small microbiome studies to analyses spanning thousands of species.</p>
  </section>

  <section class="research-section" id="fair-ai-ready-data" style="--accent:#b0792a">
    <div class="research-section-top">
      <span class="research-peak" aria-hidden="true"><svg viewBox="0 0 30 56" preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMax meet"><line class="peak-base" x1="0" y1="55" x2="30" y2="55" /><rect class="peak-bar" x="12" y="8" width="6" height="47" rx="2" /></svg></span>
      <h2 class="research-section-title">FAIR &amp; AI-ready data</h2>
    </div>
    <p class="research-section-desc">Public proteomics data only becomes scientifically reusable when it is described in a way that both people and machines can understand. We therefore work on metadata annotation, data standards, and community-facing tools that make proteomics data findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable, and, increasingly, AI-ready. This is a community effort: through PSI-AI we help shape metadata standards for AI use, through ProteomicsML we curate machine-learning-ready benchmark datasets and teach others to apply AI to proteomics, and through projects such as mzPeak and rusteomics we build fast, open tooling that eases data parsing and reuse at scale. Resources such as lesSDRF and annotation workflows around public repositories help turn deposited datasets into structured research objects that can be searched, compared, reprocessed, and used to train machine learning models. We want to move proteomics from data availability to data usability, where public datasets can support new questions, and new AI models, beyond the study for which they were originally generated.</p>
  </section>

  <section class="research-section" id="reprocessing" style="--accent:#6a4c93">
    <div class="research-section-top">
      <span class="research-peak" aria-hidden="true"><svg viewBox="0 0 30 56" preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMax meet"><line class="peak-base" x1="0" y1="55" x2="30" y2="55" /><rect class="peak-bar" x="12" y="8" width="6" height="47" rx="2" /></svg></span>
      <h2 class="research-section-title">Reprocessing</h2>
    </div>
    <p class="research-section-desc">Reprocessing brings our research lines together: peptide-property prediction improves how we model analyte behaviour, identification and rescoring software recover confident evidence from complex spectra, and FAIR annotation makes public datasets comparable at scale. We combine these developments to revisit existing proteomics data with new computational questions, extracting biological information that was not accessible in the original analysis. This includes large-scale tissue profiling, biomarker discovery, and the systematic recovery of previously missed post-translational modifications, as illustrated by Scop3PTM. Our aim is to make public proteomics data scientifically active beyond deposition, turning the vast amount of data that already exists into a continuing source of new biological insight.</p>
  </section>

</div>

<script>
  // Draw each section's peak up as it scrolls into view. Purely decorative,
  // so a reduced-motion preference just renders the final state immediately.
  (function () {
    // Smooth scrolling for the spectrum labels is handled in CSS via
    // `html { scroll-behavior: smooth }`, so native #anchor navigation is enough.
    var reduce = window.matchMedia && window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)').matches;

    var sections = document.querySelectorAll('.research-sections .research-section');
    if (!sections.length) return;
    if (reduce || !('IntersectionObserver' in window)) {
      sections.forEach(function (s) { s.classList.add('is-revealed'); });
      return;
    }
    var io = new IntersectionObserver(function (entries) {
      entries.forEach(function (e) {
        if (e.isIntersecting) { e.target.classList.add('is-revealed'); io.unobserve(e.target); }
      });
    }, { threshold: 0.35, rootMargin: '0px 0px -10% 0px' });
    sections.forEach(function (s) { io.observe(s); });
  })();
</script>
```
